metadata.title: Nondelegation Checklist: Can vs. Should
metadata.description_blurb: Capability is not authorization — the nondelegation checklist exists because 'Cowork can do this' and 'Cowork should do this' are two different sentences, and the gap between them has legal, ethical, and professional consequences.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: She asked Cowork to categorize student accommodation files by risk level so advisors would know who to prioritize. Cowork could do it. The folder was right there. Cowork can read PDFs. She did not ask the question she needed to ask first — not can Cowork do this, but should Cowork do this. The files were FERPA-protected educational records. The risk categorization was professional judgment. The data exposure happened the moment the files entered the session. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, categorize student accommodation files by risk level

props.command: Categorize these student accommodation files by risk level so advisors know who to prioritize

beats.narration_text: The wrong question is can Cowork do this. The right question is should Cowork do this. Capability and authorization are not the same. A task that Cowork is technically capable of may be one where: the data exposure is itself the problem, the judgment required is professional not clerical, the consequences are irreversible, or the institutional obligation exists regardless of AI trustworthiness. Capability does not answer any of those questions.
beats.new_visual_element: Can vs. should — capability ≠ authorization / data exposure / professional judgment / irreversibility / obligation

beats.narration_text: Seven categories that belong on the nondelegation checklist. Student educational records under FERPA. Health, legal, or financial data about identifiable individuals. Risk categorization of specific people — professional judgment, not clerical work. Irreversible external actions: send, submit, delete, publish. Credentials, authentication tokens, access keys. Decisions that affect employment, academic standing, or legal status. And any action where the data exposure is itself the harm, regardless of output quality.
beats.new_visual_element: Seven categories — FERPA / health-legal-financial / risk categorization / irreversible / credentials / status decisions / exposure harm

beats.narration_text: Three redesign moves that preserve the value while removing the problem. Reduce the data: use synthetic or anonymized examples instead of identifiable records — Cowork helps you think through the categorization criteria without seeing the real files. Reduce the action: Cowork drafts a prioritization framework, you apply it to the real files manually. Reduce the scope: Cowork processes only the aggregate data, not the individual-level records.
beats.new_visual_element: Redesign moves — reduce data: anonymized / reduce action: draft then human executes / reduce scope: aggregate not individual

beats.narration_text: The nondelegation checklist is not a limit on what Cowork can do. It is a list of questions to answer before you decide. Human review at the end does not fix a data exposure that happened at the start. The moment identifiable records enter the session, the exposure has occurred. The checklist belongs before the task brief — run it before you open the folder.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — nondelegation checklist / run before task brief / exposure at entry not at output
beats.spark_line: Run the checklist before the folder.

beats.narration_text: Run this check before any task that touches sensitive data. Paste this.

props.command: Before I delegate this task, help me check: (1) Does it involve identifiable educational, health, legal, or financial records? (2) Does it require professional judgment about specific individuals? (3) Does it include irreversible external actions — send, submit, delete, publish? (4) Is the data exposure itself a problem, regardless of output quality? If any answer is yes, suggest a redesign that reduces data, scope, or action to remove the problem — not a workaround that still processes the sensitive material.

beats.narration_text: If this stops the task that should not have been delegated, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
